cards.text: To get the length of a std::array at runtime, use the {{c1::size()}} member function
To access element at index 3 of std::array arr, use {{c1::arr[3]}} with the subscript operator

cards.front: Does the subscript operator [] perform bounds checking on std::array?
cards.back: No, operator[] does not perform bounds checking for performance reasons. Accessing out-of-bounds elements causes undefined behavior.

cards.text: To access std::array elements with bounds checking, use the {{c1::at()}} member function instead of operator[]

cards.front: What happens when you access an out-of-bounds index using std::array's at() function?
cards.back: It throws a std::out_of_range exception that can be caught and handled, preventing undefined behavior.

cards.question: What's the problem with this code?
cards.answer: Index 5 is out of bounds. Valid indices are 0-4 for an array of length 5.
cards.explanation: Array indices start at 0, so an array of length 5 has valid indices 0, 1, 2, 3, 4. Accessing index 5 causes undefined behavior.

cards.text: To iterate over all elements of a std::array arr, use a range-based for loop: {{c1::for (auto element : arr)}}

cards.front: Can you use std::array's size() function in a constant expression?
cards.back: Yes, size() is constexpr, so it can be used in compile-time contexts like array declarations or static_assert.

cards.text: The type returned by std::array's size() function is {{c1::std::size_t}}, which is an unsigned integer type

cards.concept: When to use at() vs operator[] for std::array access
cards.explanation:
Use operator[] when:
  • Performance is critical
  • You're certain the index is valid
Use at() when:
  • The index comes from user input or external sources
  • You want runtime safety and can handle exceptions
  • During development/debugging
examples: arr.at(i);  // Slower, throws on invalid index

cards.text: To access the first element of std::array arr, use {{c1::arr.front()}} and for the last element use {{c2::arr.back()}}

cards.front: Why can using size_t for loop indices with std::array be problematic?
cards.back: size_t is unsigned, which can cause issues when decrementing (wraps around to large value at 0) or when mixing with signed integers in comparisons.
